name: Río de la Vega by Santo Tomé and Peal de Becerro — circular from Quesada
name_original: Río de la Vega por Santo Tomé y Peal de Becerro — circular desde Quesada
region_intro: Road cycling around Cazorla offers diverse terrain within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. The region features mountainous landscapes with significant elevation gains, including peaks up to 1500 meters and challenging climbs. Cyclists encounter varied scenery, from dense Mediterranean and pine forests to river valleys, limestone crags, and expansive olive groves. The area is also home to the sources of the Guadalquivir and Segura rivers, contributing to scenic routes alongside hydrological features like the Tranco Reservoir. Best road cycling routes around Cazorla The most popular road cycling route is Cazorla – tour around the Natural Park of Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas, an 85.9 miles (138.3 km) trail that takes 8 hours 11 minutes to complete. This route navigates the extensive natural park, featuring substantial elevation changes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Río de la Vega by Santo Tomé and Peal de Becerro — circular from Quesada, a moderate 33.9 miles (54.5 km) path. This route offers a circular journey through the landscape surrounding Quesada, incorporating river views and local villages. Local road cyclists also love the Tranco de Beas Reservoir – Cazorla loop from Cazorla, a 90.1 miles (145.1 km) trail leading through the Tranco Reservoir area, often completed in about 7 hours 10 minutes. Road cycling around Cazorla is defined by mountainous terrain, pine forests, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate climbs to challenging long-distance routes. The routes in Cazorla are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 8 reviews. More than 700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cazorla's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Cazorla?
A: Cazorla offers a diverse network of nearly 20 road cycling routes. These routes traverse the stunning landscapes of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, providing options for various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Cazorla?
A: Road cycling in Cazorla is characterized by mountainous terrain with significant elevation gains, often featuring peaks between 1000 and 1500 meters. You'll encounter a variety of landscapes, from dense Mediterranean and pine forests to river valleys, dramatic limestone crags, and expansive olive groves. Routes can include challenging climbs like those found on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31856880" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Palomas Viewpoint loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer views of significant natural features. You can cycle past the sources of the Guadalquivir and Segura rivers, and enjoy hydrological features like the Tranco Reservoir. Specific highlights include the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6598554" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calavera Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5674304" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerrada del Utrero Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, and the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/183420" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Elías Gorge&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20124520" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tranco de Beas Reservoir – Cazorla loop&lt;/a&gt; is a great option for water views.

Q: Are there any historical sites or charming villages to explore while cycling?
A: Yes, the region is dotted with picturesque whitewashed Andalusian villages such as Cazorla itself, La Iruela, Segura de la Sierra, and Hornos de Segura. You can also spot historic landmarks like La Yedra Castle and Iruela Castle, often perched dramatically with astounding views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123426" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazorla – Castillo de La Iruela loop&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent route to experience some of these cultural gems.

Q: Are there challenging routes for experienced road cyclists?
A: Absolutely. Cazorla is known for its demanding routes with substantial elevation gains. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996055782/cazorla-tour-around-the-natural-park-of-sierra-de-cazorla-segura-y-las-villas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazorla – tour around the Natural Park of Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 85.9-mile route with over 2800 meters of ascent, navigating the extensive natural park.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Cazorla?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Cazorla are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1344749104/rio-de-la-vega-by-santo-tome-and-peal-de-becerro-circular-from-quesada" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río de la Vega by Santo Tomé and Peal de Becerro — circular from Quesada&lt;/a&gt;, which offers river views and passes through local villages.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Cazorla?
A: The region offers good cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and potential colder conditions or snow at higher elevations in winter. These seasons also showcase the diverse flora and fauna of the natural park.

Q: Are permits required for road cycling in Sierra de Cazorla Natural Park?
A: Generally, road cycling on public roads within the Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park does not require a specific permit. However, it's always advisable to check with local authorities or the park's official website for any specific regulations or temporary restrictions, especially for off-road sections or protected areas, which may not be suitable for road bikes.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Cazorla?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in Cazorla town and other villages within the natural park. Many routes start from these locations, offering convenient access. It's recommended to look for designated parking areas, especially if you plan for a longer ride.

Q: Can I use public transport to reach cycling routes with my bike in Cazorla?
A: Public transport options, such as ALSA buses, connect various towns in and around Cazorla. While some services may allow bikes, it's crucial to check their specific policies regarding bicycle transport, including any size restrictions or additional fees, and if advance booking is required. This can vary by route and bus type.

Q: Where can I find refreshments or places to eat along the routes?
A: The charming villages scattered throughout the Cazorla region, such as Cazorla, La Iruela, and Hornos de Segura, offer numerous cafes, restaurants, and local shops where you can refuel. The area is known for its excellent traditional Andalusian cuisine, featuring locally-sourced ingredients, perfect for a post-ride meal.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Cazorla?
A: The road cycling experience in Cazorla is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists frequently praise the region's diverse and stunning scenery, the challenging yet rewarding climbs, and the quiet mountain roads that allow for an immersive outdoor experience within the vast natural park.
tips: Santo Tomé is located on the slopes of the Sierra de Cazorla, in the valley of the Guadalquivir river. passing the center of the village you can see the bell tower of the stone church, dating from the fourteenth century.

La Yedra Castle is an ancient defensive structure in the Spanish municipality of Cazorla. It is located at the foot of the Salvatierra hill, above the Cerezuelo river, in the province of Jaén. It is a building from the Berber period, but it was completed in its current form by the Castilians. From the complex, located 831 meters above sea level, you can see the city of Cazorla and its surroundings.

The architecture has several Muslim features, although over the centuries Christians completed the final design, so the Gothic style predominates (translated from Wikipedia).
